topic: Sunday service reflection — sermon on compassion

turns.content: Good evening. I have been sitting with something from this morning's service and I think articulating it would help me process it more fully.
I'd be glad to help you think through it. What was on your mind from the service?

The sermon was on compassion. The pastor drew from the parable of the Good Samaritan, which I have heard countless times over the years. But today he framed it in a way that caught me off guard. He said that the priest and the Levite who passed by the injured man were not necessarily indifferent. They may have been exhausted. They may have had obligations waiting. And yet the Samaritan stopped. The question was not whether you feel compassion but whether you act on it when the cost is real.

That's a striking reframing. It moves the emphasis from the moral failing of the ones who passed by to the active choice of the one who stopped. The idea that compassion is costly and requires a decision, not just a feeling, adds a lot of weight to it. What was it about that framing that stayed with you?

I think it is because I live in that tension every day. In my work, I am constantly making decisions about who receives my time and attention. There are patients I can help and patients whose conditions are beyond what surgery can offer. And there are moments when I am so fatigued that my capacity for genuine compassion is diminished. The body is willing but the spirit, as they say, grows weary. Or perhaps it is the reverse.

That's a deeply honest reflection. The reverse formulation is interesting too — the spirit may be willing but the body's exhaustion limits what you can actually give. In a field like yours, compassion fatigue is a real phenomenon, and recognizing it isn't a weakness. It sounds like the sermon touched something about the gap between your intention and your capacity on difficult days.

Yes, precisely. And what struck me further was this. The Samaritan was a traveler. He was on his way somewhere. He had his own purpose and destination. He chose to interrupt his plan. In surgery, you do not have the luxury of interruption. Everything is planned and precise. But outside the operating room, in the corridors, in the conversations with families, that is where the interruptions come. A wife who needs five more minutes to ask questions. A resident who is struggling and needs guidance rather than a directive. Those are the Samaritan moments.

That's a beautiful connection. The operating room is structured by necessity — there's no room for spontaneity when you're working with that kind of precision. But the human moments outside of it are exactly where that deliberate choice to stop and be present matters most. And the fact that you recognize those hallway conversations and extra five minutes with a family member as acts of compassion, not just professional obligation, says something about how you approach your work. Do you find it difficult to make that shift between the precision of surgery and the openness those moments require?

It is a shift, yes. In the operating theater, empathy is a liability. You cannot afford to think about the person's grandchildren while you are dissecting tissue near the brainstem. You must be utterly technical. But then you walk out and that same family is waiting, and they need you to be human again. Some of my colleagues struggle with that transition. I have been, by God's grace, reasonably able to make it. But on the days when I am most tired, I notice I am slower to shift. I become efficient with families rather than present.

"Efficient rather than present" — that's a remarkably self-aware distinction. Efficiency in that context looks like competence on the outside but can feel hollow to the people on the receiving end. The fact that you can identify when you're in that mode suggests you haven't lost the compass, even when fatigue pulls you toward it. Is there anything you do to recalibrate on those harder days?

I pray before I leave the surgical suite. It is a brief prayer, nothing elaborate. I ask for the grace to see the person waiting for me as God sees them. It does not always work, in the sense that I do not always feel a transformation. But it is a discipline. It orients me. And sometimes, that orientation is enough to carry me through the conversation with the tenderness that is required.

That's a powerful practice. It sounds less like a request for a feeling and more like a deliberate act of positioning yourself — choosing to approach the next moment with a particular posture, even if the emotion doesn't fully follow. That discipline itself is a form of compassion, isn't it? Not waiting until you feel compassionate, but choosing to act as if you are until your heart catches up.

Ah but that is exactly what the pastor was saying this morning. Compassion as action, not sentiment. You have helped me close the circle on this. I came in with the sermon sitting uneasily in my chest and now I understand why. It was not convicting me of failure. It was reminding me that the discipline I already practice is, in fact, the thing itself. The stopping. The turning toward the person. Even when you are tired. Even when you have somewhere to be.

That's a meaningful realization. The sermon wasn't asking you to do more — it was affirming what you already do and reminding you why it matters. The fact that you pause and pray before meeting families, that you notice when you're being efficient instead of present, that you give those extra minutes even when you're exhausted — that is the Samaritan stopping. I'm glad you took the time to sit with it and talk it through.
Thank you. This has been a clarifying conversation. I will carry this into the week ahead. There is a full surgical schedule and I know the fatigue will come. But the discipline remains. Good night.
Good night. I hope the week ahead is sustaining, both in the work and in the moments between. Take care of yourself as well as you take care of others.
